summary: DHH argues that privacy scandals are fundamentally caused by the economic value of personal information in ad targeting. Drawing an analogy to ivory poaching, he contends that eliminating demand for targeted advertising would eliminate the incentive to harvest personal data. He points out that advertising worked fine before personal targeting existed, relying on contextual placement instead. He calls for regulation banning ad targeting by personal information, noting precedents in tobacco and children's advertising restrictions.
key_points: Privacy scandals are demand-driven: personal data is harvested because ad targeting makes it valuable, just as elephants are poached because ivory has a market
Contextual advertising (placing ads based on content rather than personal data) worked before and may be equally effective, citing the NYT's post-GDPR experience
The societal cost of allowing personally targeted ads far outweighs any efficiency gains
Advertising regulation has clear precedent (tobacco, children's advertising) and works effectively
money_quotes: Elephants wouldn't be killed for their tusks if there wasn't a demand for ivory.
Personal information is valuable because we use it to target ads.
But what if you couldn't do that? Then the personal information would cease to have value, and the flood of privacy scandals would stop.
The world of commerce spun around just fine in the era before ads could be targeted by personal information.
The societal price we pay for allowing ads to be targeted is far too high.
Just try to imagine that world without ad targeting. It's hard to imagine that it wouldn't be a better one.
key_insight: The root cause of privacy scandals isn't bad actors but the economic incentive created by ad targeting — ban targeted advertising and the reason to harvest personal data disappears.
